3: 
4: package terraform
5: 
6: import (
7: 	"github.com/zclconf/go-cty/cty"
8: 
9: 	"github.com/hashicorp/terraform/internal/addrs"
10: 	"github.com/hashicorp/terraform/internal/plans"
11: 	"github.com/hashicorp/terraform/internal/providers"
12: 	"github.com/hashicorp/terraform/internal/states"
13: )
14: 
15: // HookAction is an enum of actions that can be taken as a result of a hook
16: // callback. This allows you to modify the behavior of Terraform at runtime.
17: type HookAction byte
18: 
19: const (
20: 	// HookActionContinue continues with processing as usual.
21: 	HookActionContinue HookAction = iota
22: 
23: 	// HookActionHalt halts immediately: no more hooks are processed
24: 	// and the action that Terraform was about to take is cancelled.
25: 	HookActionHalt
26: )
27: 
28: // HookResourceIdentity is passed to Hook interface methods to fully identify
29: // the resource instance being operated on. It currently includes the resource
30: // address and the provider address.
31: type HookResourceIdentity struct {
32: 	Addr         addrs.AbsResourceInstance
33: 	ProviderAddr addrs.Provider
34: }
35: 
36: // HookActionIdentity is passed to Hook interface methods to fully identify
37: // the action being performed.
38: type HookActionIdentity struct {
39: 	Addr addrs.AbsActionInstance
40: 
41: 	ActionTrigger plans.ActionTrigger
42: }
43: 
44: func (i *HookActionIdentity) String() string {
45: 	return i.Addr.String() + " (triggered by " + i.ActionTrigger.String() + ")"
46: }
47: 
48: // Hook is the interface that must be implemented to hook into various
49: // parts of Terraform, allowing you to inspect or change behavior at runtime.
50: //
51: // There are MANY hook points into Terraform. If you only want to implement
52: // some hook points, but not all (which is the likely case), then embed the
53: // NilHook into your struct, which implements all of the interface but does
54: // nothing. Then, override only the functions you want to implement.
55: type Hook interface {
56: 	// PreApply and PostApply are called before and after an action for a
57: 	// single instance is applied. The error argument in PostApply is the
58: 	// error, if any, that was returned from the provider Apply call itself.
59: 	PreApply(id HookResourceIdentity, dk addrs.DeposedKey, action plans.Action, priorState, plannedNewState cty.Value) (HookAction, error)
60: 	PostApply(id HookResourceIdentity, dk addrs.DeposedKey, newState cty.Value, err error) (HookAction, error)
61: 
62: 	// PreDiff and PostDiff are called before and after a provider is given
63: 	// the opportunity to customize the proposed new state to produce the
64: 	// planned new state.
65: 	PreDiff(id HookResourceIdentity, dk addrs.DeposedKey, priorState, proposedNewState cty.Value) (HookAction, error)
66: 	PostDiff(id HookResourceIdentity, dk addrs.DeposedKey, action plans.Action, priorState, plannedNewState cty.Value) (HookAction, error)
67: 
68: 	// The provisioning hooks signal both the overall start end end of
69: 	// provisioning for a particular instance and of each of the individual
70: 	// configured provisioners for each instance. The sequence of these
71: 	// for a given instance might look something like this:
72: 	//
73: 	//          PreProvisionInstance(aws_instance.foo[1], ...)
74: 	//      PreProvisionInstanceStep(aws_instance.foo[1], "file")
75: 	//     PostProvisionInstanceStep(aws_instance.foo[1], "file", nil)
76: 	//      PreProvisionInstanceStep(aws_instance.foo[1], "remote-exec")
77: 	//               ProvisionOutput(aws_instance.foo[1], "remote-exec", "Installing foo...")
78: 	//               ProvisionOutput(aws_instance.foo[1], "remote-exec", "Configuring bar...")
79: 	//     PostProvisionInstanceStep(aws_instance.foo[1], "remote-exec", nil)
80: 	//         PostProvisionInstance(aws_instance.foo[1], ...)
81: 	//
82: 	// ProvisionOutput is called with output sent back by the provisioners.
83: 	// This will be called multiple times as output comes in, with each call
84: 	// representing one line of output. It cannot control whether the
85: 	// provisioner continues running.
86: 	PreProvisionInstance(id HookResourceIdentity, state cty.Value) (HookAction, error)
87: 	PostProvisionInstance(id HookResourceIdentity, state cty.Value) (HookAction, error)
88: 	PreProvisionInstanceStep(id HookResourceIdentity, typeName string) (HookAction, error)
89: 	PostProvisionInstanceStep(id HookResourceIdentity, typeName string, err error) (HookAction, error)
90: 	ProvisionOutput(id HookResourceIdentity, typeName string, line string)
91: 
92: 	// PreRefresh and PostRefresh are called before and after a single
93: 	// resource state is refreshed, respectively.
94: 	PreRefresh(id HookResourceIdentity, dk addrs.DeposedKey, priorState cty.Value) (HookAction, error)
95: 	PostRefresh(id HookResourceIdentity, dk addrs.DeposedKey, priorState cty.Value, newState cty.Value) (HookAction, error)
96: 
97: 	// PreImportState and PostImportState are called before and after
98: 	// (respectively) each state import operation for a given resource address when
99: 	// using the legacy import command.
100: 	PreImportState(id HookResourceIdentity, importID string) (HookAction, error)
101: 	PostImportState(id HookResourceIdentity, imported []providers.ImportedResource) (HookAction, error)
102: 
103: 	// PrePlanImport and PostPlanImport are called during a plan before and after planning to import
104: 	// a new resource using the configuration-driven import workflow.
105: 	PrePlanImport(id HookResourceIdentity, importTarget cty.Value) (HookAction, error)
106: 	PostPlanImport(id HookResourceIdentity, imported []providers.ImportedResource) (HookAction, error)
107: 
108: 	// PreApplyImport and PostApplyImport are called during an apply for each imported resource when
109: 	// using the configuration-driven import workflow.
110: 	PreApplyImport(id HookResourceIdentity, importing plans.ImportingSrc) (HookAction, error)
111: 	PostApplyImport(id HookResourceIdentity, importing plans.ImportingSrc) (HookAction, error)
112: 
113: 	// PreEphemeralOp and PostEphemeralOp are called during an operation on ephemeral resource
114: 	// such as opening, renewal or closing
115: 	PreEphemeralOp(id HookResourceIdentity, action plans.Action) (HookAction, error)
116: 	PostEphemeralOp(id HookResourceIdentity, action plans.Action, opErr error) (HookAction, error)
117: 
118: 	// PreListQuery and PostListQuery are called during a query operation before and after
119: 	// resources are queried from the provider.
120: 	PreListQuery(id HookResourceIdentity, inputConfig cty.Value) (HookAction, error)
121: 	PostListQuery(id HookResourceIdentity, results plans.QueryResults, identityVersion int64) (HookAction, error)
122: 
123: 	// StartAction, ProgressAction, and CompleteAction are called during the
124: 	// lifecycle of an action invocation.
125: 	StartAction(id HookActionIdentity) (HookAction, error)
126: 	ProgressAction(id HookActionIdentity, progress string) (HookAction, error)
127: 	CompleteAction(id HookActionIdentity, err error) (HookAction, error)
128: 
129: 	// Stopping is called if an external signal requests that Terraform
130: 	// gracefully abort an operation in progress.
131: 	//
132: 	// This notification might suggest that the user wants Terraform to exit
133: 	// ASAP and in that case it's possible that if Terraform runs for too much
134: 	// longer then it'll get killed un-gracefully, and so this hook could be
135: 	// an opportunity to persist any transient data that would be lost under
136: 	// a subsequent kill signal. However, implementations must take care to do
137: 	// so in a way that won't cause corruption if the process _is_ killed while
138: 	// this hook is still running.
139: 	//
140: 	// This hook cannot control whether Terraform continues, because the
141: 	// graceful shutdown process is typically already running by the time this
142: 	// function is called.
143: 	Stopping()
144: 
145: 	// PostStateUpdate is called each time the state is updated. The caller must
146: 	// coordinate a lock for the state if necessary, such that the Hook may
147: 	// access it freely without any need for additional locks to protect from
148: 	// concurrent writes. Implementations which modify or retain the state after
149: 	// the call has returned must copy the state.
150: 	PostStateUpdate(new *states.State) (HookAction, error)
151: }

3: 
4: package terraform
5: 
6: import (
7: 	"log"
8: 	"time"
9: 
10: 	"github.com/hashicorp/terraform/internal/actions"
11: 	"github.com/hashicorp/terraform/internal/addrs"
12: 	"github.com/hashicorp/terraform/internal/checks"
13: 	"github.com/hashicorp/terraform/internal/configs"
14: 	"github.com/hashicorp/terraform/internal/instances"
15: 	"github.com/hashicorp/terraform/internal/lang"
16: 	"github.com/hashicorp/terraform/internal/moduletest/mocking"
17: 	"github.com/hashicorp/terraform/internal/namedvals"
18: 	"github.com/hashicorp/terraform/internal/plans"
19: 	"github.com/hashicorp/terraform/internal/plans/deferring"
20: 	"github.com/hashicorp/terraform/internal/providers"
21: 	"github.com/hashicorp/terraform/internal/refactoring"
22: 	"github.com/hashicorp/terraform/internal/resources/ephemeral"
23: 	"github.com/hashicorp/terraform/internal/states"
24: 	"github.com/hashicorp/terraform/internal/tfdiags"
25: )
26: 
27: // graphWalkOpts captures some transient values we use (and possibly mutate)
28: // during a graph walk.
29: //
30: // The way these options get used unfortunately varies between the different
31: // walkOperation types. This is a historical design wart that dates back to
32: // us using the same graph structure for all operations; hopefully we'll
33: // make the necessary differences between the walk types more explicit someday.
34: type graphWalkOpts struct {
35: 	InputState *states.State
36: 	Changes    *plans.Changes
37: 	Config     *configs.Config
38: 
39: 	// ExternalProviderConfigs is used for walks that make use of configured
40: 	// providers (e.g. plan and apply) to satisfy situations where the root
41: 	// module itself declares that it expects to have providers passed in
42: 	// from outside.
43: 	//
44: 	// This should not be populated for walks that use only unconfigured
45: 	// providers, such as validate. Populating it for those walks might cause
46: 	// strange things to happen, because our graph walking machinery doesn't
47: 	// always take into account what walk type it's dealing with.
48: 	ExternalProviderConfigs map[addrs.RootProviderConfig]providers.Interface
49: 
50: 	// DeferralAlowed indicates that the current runtime supports deferred actions.
51: 	DeferralAllowed bool
52: 
53: 	// ExternalDependencyDeferred indicates that something that this entire
54: 	// configuration depends on (outside the view of this modules runtime)
55: 	// has deferred changes, and therefore we must treat _all_ actions
56: 	// as deferred to produce the correct overall dependency ordering.
57: 	ExternalDependencyDeferred bool
58: 
59: 	// PlanTimeCheckResults should be populated during the apply phase with
60: 	// the snapshot of check results that was generated during the plan step.
61: 	//
62: 	// This then propagates the decisions about which checkable objects exist
63: 	// from the plan phase into the apply phase without having to re-compute
64: 	// the module and resource expansion.
65: 	PlanTimeCheckResults *states.CheckResults
66: 
67: 	// PlanTimeTimestamp should be populated during the plan phase by retrieving
68: 	// the current UTC timestamp, and should be read from the plan file during
69: 	// the apply phase.
70: 	PlanTimeTimestamp time.Time
71: 
72: 	// Overrides contains the set of overrides we should apply during this
73: 	// operation.
74: 	Overrides *mocking.Overrides
75: 
76: 	MoveResults refactoring.MoveResults
77: 
78: 	FunctionResults *lang.FunctionResults
79: 
80: 	// Forget if set to true will cause the plan to forget all resources. This is
81: 	// only allowd in the context of a destroy plan.
82: 	Forget bool
83: }
84: 
85: func (c *Context) walk(graph *Graph, operation walkOperation, opts *graphWalkOpts) (*ContextGraphWalker, tfdiags.Diagnostics) {
86: 	log.Printf("[DEBUG] Starting graph walk: %s", operation.String())
87: 
88: 	walker := c.graphWalker(graph, operation, opts)
89: 
90: 	// Watch for a stop so we can call the provider Stop() API.
91: 	watchStop, watchWait := c.watchStop(walker)
92: 
93: 	// Walk the real graph, this will block until it completes
94: 	diags := graph.Walk(walker)
95: 
96: 	// Close the channel so the watcher stops, and wait for it to return.
97: 	close(watchStop)
98: 	<-watchWait
99: 
100: 	return walker, diags
101: }
102: 
103: func (c *Context) graphWalker(graph *Graph, operation walkOperation, opts *graphWalkOpts) *ContextGraphWalker {
104: 	var state *states.SyncState
105: 	var refreshState *states.SyncState
106: 	var prevRunState *states.SyncState
107: 
108: 	// NOTE: None of the SyncState objects must directly wrap opts.InputState,
109: 	// because we use those to mutate the state object and opts.InputState
110: 	// belongs to our caller and thus we must treat it as immutable.
111: 	//
112: 	// To account for that, most of our SyncState values created below end up
113: 	// wrapping a _deep copy_ of opts.InputState instead.
114: 	inputState := opts.InputState
115: 	if inputState == nil {
116: 		// Lots of callers use nil to represent the "empty" case where we've
117: 		// not run Apply yet, so we tolerate that.
118: 		inputState = states.NewState()
119: 	}
120: 
121: 	switch operation {
122: 	case walkValidate:
123: 		// validate should not use any state
124: 		state = states.NewState().SyncWrapper()
125: 
126: 		// validate currently uses the plan graph, so we have to populate the
127: 		// refreshState and the prevRunState.
128: 		refreshState = states.NewState().SyncWrapper()
129: 		prevRunState = states.NewState().SyncWrapper()
130: 
131: 	case walkPlan, walkPlanDestroy, walkImport:
132: 		state = inputState.DeepCopy().SyncWrapper()
133: 		refreshState = inputState.DeepCopy().SyncWrapper()
134: 		prevRunState = inputState.DeepCopy().SyncWrapper()
135: 
136: 		// For both of our new states we'll discard the previous run's
137: 		// check results, since we can still refer to them from the
138: 		// prevRunState object if we need to.
139: 		state.DiscardCheckResults()
140: 		refreshState.DiscardCheckResults()
141: 
142: 	default:
143: 		state = inputState.DeepCopy().SyncWrapper()
144: 		// Only plan-like walks use refreshState and prevRunState
145: 
146: 		// Discard the input state's check results, because we should create
147: 		// a new set as a result of the graph walk.
148: 		state.DiscardCheckResults()
149: 	}
150: 
151: 	changes := opts.Changes
152: 	if changes == nil {
153: 		// Several of our non-plan walks end up sharing codepaths with the
154: 		// plan walk and thus expect to generate planned changes even though
155: 		// we don't care about them. To avoid those crashing, we'll just
156: 		// insert a placeholder changes object which'll get discarded
157: 		// afterwards.
158: 		changes = plans.NewChanges()
159: 	}
160: 
161: 	if opts.Config == nil {
162: 		panic("Context.graphWalker call without Config")
163: 	}
164: 
165: 	checkState := checks.NewState(opts.Config)
166: 	if opts.PlanTimeCheckResults != nil {
167: 		// We'll re-report all of the same objects we determined during the
168: 		// plan phase so that we can repeat the checks during the apply
169: 		// phase to finalize them.
170: 		for _, configElem := range opts.PlanTimeCheckResults.ConfigResults.Elems {
171: 			if configElem.Value.ObjectAddrsKnown() {
172: 				configAddr := configElem.Key
173: 				checkState.ReportCheckableObjects(configAddr, configElem.Value.ObjectResults.Keys())
174: 			}
175: 		}
176: 	}
177: 
178: 	deferred := deferring.NewDeferred(opts.DeferralAllowed)
179: 	if opts.ExternalDependencyDeferred {
180: 		deferred.SetExternalDependencyDeferred()
181: 	}
182: 
183: 	return &ContextGraphWalker{
184: 		Context:                 c,
185: 		State:                   state,
186: 		Config:                  opts.Config,
187: 		RefreshState:            refreshState,
188: 		Overrides:               opts.Overrides,
189: 		PrevRunState:            prevRunState,
190: 		Changes:                 changes.SyncWrapper(),
191: 		NamedValues:             namedvals.NewState(),
192: 		EphemeralResources:      ephemeral.NewResources(),
193: 		Deferrals:               deferred,
194: 		Checks:                  checkState,
195: 		InstanceExpander:        instances.NewExpander(opts.Overrides),
196: 		ExternalProviderConfigs: opts.ExternalProviderConfigs,
197: 		MoveResults:             opts.MoveResults,
198: 		Operation:               operation,
199: 		StopContext:             c.runContext,
200: 		PlanTimestamp:           opts.PlanTimeTimestamp,
201: 		functionResults:         opts.FunctionResults,
202: 		Forget:                  opts.Forget,
203: 		Actions:                 actions.NewActions(),
204: 	}
205: }

{"content":"1: // Copyright (c) HashiCorp, Inc.
2: // SPDX-License-Identifier: BUSL-1.1
3: 
4: package terraform
5: 
6: import (
7: 	"context"
8: 	"sync"
9: 	"time"
10: 
11: 	"github.com/hashicorp/terraform/internal/actions"
12: 	"github.com/hashicorp/terraform/internal/addrs"
13: 	"github.com/hashicorp/terraform/internal/checks"
14: 	"github.com/hashicorp/terraform/internal/collections"
15: 	"github.com/hashicorp/terraform/internal/configs"
16: 	"github.com/hashicorp/terraform/internal/configs/configschema"
17: 	"github.com/hashicorp/terraform/internal/instances"
18: 	"github.com/hashicorp/terraform/internal/lang"
19: 	"github.com/hashicorp/terraform/internal/moduletest/mocking"
20: 	"github.com/hashicorp/terraform/internal/namedvals"
21: 	"github.com/hashicorp/terraform/internal/plans"
22: 	"github.com/hashicorp/terraform/internal/plans/deferring"
23: 	"github.com/hashicorp/terraform/internal/providers"
24: 	"github.com/hashicorp/terraform/internal/provisioners"
25: 	"github.com/hashicorp/terraform/internal/refactoring"
26: 	"github.com/hashicorp/terraform/internal/resources/ephemeral"
27: 	"github.com/hashicorp/terraform/internal/states"
28: 	"github.com/hashicorp/terraform/internal/tfdiags"
29: )
30: 
31: // ContextGraphWalker is the GraphWalker implementation used with the
32: // Context struct to walk and evaluate the graph.
33: type ContextGraphWalker struct {
34: 	NullGraphWalker
35: 
36: 	// Configurable values
37: 	Context                 *Context
38: 	State                   *states.SyncState    // Used for safe concurrent access to state
39: 	RefreshState            *states.SyncState    // Used for safe concurrent access to state
40: 	PrevRunState            *states.SyncState    // Used for safe concurrent access to state
41: 	Changes                 *plans.ChangesSync   // Used for safe concurrent writes to changes
42: 	Checks                  *checks.State        // Used for safe concurrent writes of checkable objects and their check results
43: 	NamedValues             *namedvals.State     // Tracks evaluation of input variables, local values, and output values
44: 	InstanceExpander        *instances.Expander  // Tracks our gradual expansion of module and resource instances
45: 	Deferrals               *deferring.Deferred  // Tracks any deferred actions
46: 	EphemeralResources      *ephemeral.Resources // Tracks active instances of ephemeral resources
47: 	Imports                 []configs.Import
48: 	MoveResults             refactoring.MoveResults // Read-only record of earlier processing of move statements
49: 	Operation               walkOperation
50: 	StopContext             context.Context
51: 	ExternalProviderConfigs map[addrs.RootProviderConfig]providers.Interface
52: 	Config                  *configs.Config
53: 	PlanTimestamp           time.Time
54: 	Overrides               *mocking.Overrides
55: 	// Forget if set to true will cause the plan to forget all resources. This is
56: 	// only allowd in the context of a destroy plan.
57: 	Forget  bool
58: 	Actions *actions.Actions
59: 
60: 	// This is an output. Do not set this, nor read it while a graph walk
61: 	// is in progress.
62: 	NonFatalDiagnostics tfdiags.Diagnostics
63: 
64: 	once               sync.Once
65: 	contexts           collections.Map[evalContextScope, *BuiltinEvalContext]
66: 	contextLock        sync.Mutex
67: 	providerCache      map[string]providers.Interface
68: 	providerFuncCache  map[string]providers.Interface
69: 	functionResults    *lang.FunctionResults
70: 	providerSchemas    map[string]providers.ProviderSchema
71: 	providerLock       sync.Mutex
72: 	provisionerCache   map[string]provisioners.Interface
73: 	provisionerSchemas map[string]*configschema.Block
74: 	provisionerLock    sync.Mutex
75: }
76: 
77: var _ GraphWalker = (*ContextGraphWalker)(nil)
78: 
79: // enterScope provides an EvalContext associated with the given scope.
80: func (w *ContextGraphWalker) enterScope(scope evalContextScope) EvalContext {
81: 	if scope == nil {
82: 		// Just want a global EvalContext then, presumably.
83: 		return w.EvalContext()
84: 	}
85: 
86: 	w.contextLock.Lock()
87: 	defer w.contextLock.Unlock()
88: 
89: 	// We might already have a context for this scope.
90: 	if ctx, ok := w.contexts.GetOk(scope); ok {
91: 		return ctx
92: 	}
93: 
94: 	ctx := w.EvalContext().withScope(scope).(*BuiltinEvalContext)
95: 	w.contexts.Put(scope, ctx)
96: 	return ctx
97: }
98: 
99: func (w *ContextGraphWalker) EvalContext() EvalContext {
100: 	w.once.Do(w.init)
101: 
102: 	// Our evaluator shares some locks with the main context and the walker
103: 	// so that we can safely run multiple evaluations at once across
104: 	// different modules.
105: 	evaluator := &Evaluator{
106: 		Meta:               w.Context.meta,
107: 		Config:             w.Config,
108: 		Operation:          w.Operation,
109: 		State:              w.State,
110: 		Changes:            w.Changes,
111: 		EphemeralResources: w.EphemeralResources,
112: 		Plugins:            w.Context.plugins,
113: 		Instances:          w.InstanceExpander,
114: 		NamedValues:        w.NamedValues,
115: 		Deferrals:          w.Deferrals,
116: 		PlanTimestamp:      w.PlanTimestamp,
117: 		FunctionResults:    w.functionResults,
118: 	}
119: 
120: 	ctx := &BuiltinEvalContext{
121: 		StopContext:             w.StopContext,
122: 		Hooks:                   w.Context.hooks,
123: 		InputValue:              w.Context.uiInput,
124: 		EphemeralResourcesValue: w.EphemeralResources,
125: 		InstanceExpanderValue:   w.InstanceExpander,
126: 		Plugins:                 w.Context.plugins,
127: 		ExternalProviderConfigs: w.ExternalProviderConfigs,
128: 		MoveResultsValue:        w.MoveResults,
129: 		ProviderCache:           w.providerCache,
130: 		ProviderFuncCache:       w.providerFuncCache,
131: 		FunctionResults:         w.functionResults,
132: 		ProviderInputConfig:     w.Context.providerInputConfig,
133: 		ProviderLock:            &w.providerLock,
134: 		ProvisionerCache:        w.provisionerCache,
135: 		ProvisionerLock:         &w.provisionerLock,
136: 		ChangesValue:            w.Changes,
137: 		ChecksValue:             w.Checks,
138: 		NamedValuesValue:        w.NamedValues,
139: 		DeferralsValue:          w.Deferrals,
140: 		StateValue:              w.State,
141: 		RefreshStateValue:       w.RefreshState,
142: 		PrevRunStateValue:       w.PrevRunState,
143: 		Evaluator:               evaluator,
144: 		OverrideValues:          w.Overrides,
145: 		forget:                  w.Forget,
146: 		ActionsValue:            w.Actions,
147: 	}
148: 
149: 	return ctx
150: }
151: 
152: func (w *ContextGraphWalker) init() {
153: 	w.contexts = collections.NewMap[evalContextScope, *BuiltinEvalContext]()
154: 	w.providerCache = make(map[string]providers.Interface)
155: 	w.providerFuncCache = make(map[string]providers.Interface)
156: 	w.providerSchemas = make(map[string]providers.ProviderSchema)
157: 	w.provisionerCache = make(map[string]provisioners.Interface)
158: 	w.provisionerSchemas = make(map[string]*configschema.Block)
159: }
160: 
161: func (w *ContextGraphWalker) Execute(ctx EvalContext, n GraphNodeExecutable) tfdiags.Diagnostics {
162: 	// Acquire a lock on the semaphore
163: 	w.Context.parallelSem.Acquire()
164: 	defer w.Context.parallelSem.Release()
165: 
166: 	return n.Execute(ctx, w.Operation)
167: }

{"content":"1: // Copyright (c) HashiCorp, Inc.
2: // SPDX-License-Identifier: BUSL-1.1
3: 
4: package terraform
5: 
6: import (
7: 	"fmt"
8: 	"log"
9: 
10: 	"github.com/zclconf/go-cty/cty"
11: 
12: 	"github.com/hashicorp/terraform/internal/addrs"
13: 	"github.com/hashicorp/terraform/internal/collections"
14: 	"github.com/hashicorp/terraform/internal/configs"
15: 	"github.com/hashicorp/terraform/internal/lang"
16: 	"github.com/hashicorp/terraform/internal/plans"
17: 	"github.com/hashicorp/terraform/internal/providers"
18: 	"github.com/hashicorp/terraform/internal/states"
19: 	"github.com/hashicorp/terraform/internal/tfdiags"
20: )
21: 
22: // ApplyOpts are options that affect the details of how Terraform will apply a
23: // previously-generated plan.
24: type ApplyOpts struct {
25: 	// ExternalProviders is a set of pre-configured provider instances with
26: 	// the same purpose as [PlanOpts.ExternalProviders].
27: 	//
28: 	// Callers must pass providers that are configured in a similar way as
29: 	// the providers that were passed when creating the plan that's being
30: 	// applied, or the results will be erratic.
31: 	ExternalProviders map[addrs.RootProviderConfig]providers.Interface
32: 
33: 	// SetVariables are the raw values for root module variables as provided
34: 	// by the user who is requesting the run, prior to any normalization or
35: 	// substitution of defaults. See the documentation for the InputValue
36: 	// type for more information on how to correctly populate this.
37: 	//
38: 	// During the apply phase it's only valid to specify values for input
39: 	// values that were declared as ephemeral, because all other input
40: 	// values must retain the values that were specified during planning.
41: 	SetVariables InputValues
42: 
43: 	// AllowRootEphemeralOutputs overrides a specific check made within the
44: 	// output nodes that they cannot be ephemeral at within root modules. This
45: 	// should be set to true for plans executing from within either the stacks
46: 	// or test runtimes, where the root modules as Terraform sees them aren't
47: 	// the actual root modules.
48: 	AllowRootEphemeralOutputs bool
49: }
50: 
51: // ApplyOpts creates an [ApplyOpts] with copies of all of the elements that
52: // are expected to propagate from plan to apply when planning and applying
53: // in the same process.
54: //
55: // In practice planning and applying are often separated into two different
56: // executions, in which case callers must retain enough information between
57: // plan and apply to construct an equivalent [ApplyOpts] themselves without
58: // using this function. This is here mainly for convenient internal use such
59: // as in test cases.
60: func (po *PlanOpts) ApplyOpts() *ApplyOpts {
61: 	return &ApplyOpts{
62: 		ExternalProviders:         po.ExternalProviders,
63: 		AllowRootEphemeralOutputs: po.AllowRootEphemeralOutputs,
64: 	}
65: }
66: 
67: // Apply performs the actions described by the given Plan object and returns
68: // the resulting updated state.
69: //
70: // The given configuration *must* be the same configuration that was passed
71: // earlier to Context.Plan in order to create this plan.
72: //
73: // Even if the returned diagnostics contains errors, Apply always returns the
74: // resulting state which is likely to have been partially-updated.
75: //
76: // The [opts] argument may be nil to indicate that no special options are
77: // required. In that case, Apply will use a default set of options. Some
78: // options in [PlanOpts] when creating a plan must be echoed with equivalent
79: // settings during apply, so leaving opts as nil might not be valid for
80: // certain combinations of plan-time options.
81: func (c *Context) Apply(plan *plans.Plan, config *configs.Config, opts *ApplyOpts) (*states.State, tfdiags.Diagnostics) {
82: 	state, _, diags := c.ApplyAndEval(plan, config, opts)
83: 	return state, diags
84: }
85: 
86: // ApplyAndEval is like [Context.Apply] except that it additionally makes a
87: // best effort to return a [lang.Scope] which can evaluate expressions in the
88: // root module based on the content of the new state.
89: //
90: // The scope will be nil if the apply process doesn't complete successfully
91: // enough to produce a valid evaluation scope. If the returned state is nil
92: // then the scope will always be nil, but it's also possible for the scope
93: // to be nil even when the state isn't, if the apply didn't complete enough for
94: // the evaluation scope to produce consistent results.
95: func (c *Context) ApplyAndEval(plan *plans.Plan, config *configs.Config, opts *ApplyOpts) (*states.State, *lang.Scope, tfdiags.Diagnostics) {
96: 	defer c.acquireRun("apply")()
97: 	var diags tfdiags.Diagnostics
98: 
99: 	if plan == nil {
100: 		panic("cannot apply nil plan")
101: 	}
102: 	log.Printf("[DEBUG] Building and walking apply graph for %s plan", plan.UIMode)
103: 
104: 	if opts == nil {
105: 		opts = &ApplyOpts{}
106: 	}
107: 
108: 	if plan.Errored {
109: 		diags = diags.Append(tfdiags.Sourceless(
110: 			tfdiags.Error,
111: 			"Cannot apply failed plan",
112: 			`The given plan is incomplete due to errors during planning, and so it cannot be applied.`,
113: 		))
114: 		return nil, nil, diags
115: 	}
116: 	if !plan.Applyable {
117: 		if plan.Changes.Empty() {
118: 			// If a plan is not applyable but it didn't have any errors then that
119: 			// suggests it was a "no-op" plan, which doesn't really do any
120: 			// harm to apply, so we'll just do it but leave ourselves a note
121: 			// in the trace log in case it ends up relevant to a bug report.
122: 			log.Printf("[TRACE] Applying a no-op plan")
123: 		} else {
124: 			// This situation isn't something we expect, since our own rules
125: 			// for what "applyable" means make this scenario impossible. We'll
126: 			// reject it on the assumption that something very strange is
127: 			// going on. and so better to halt than do something incorrect.
128: 			// This error message is generic and useless because we don't
129: 			// expect anyone to ever see it in normal use.
130: 			diags = diags.Append(tfdiags.Sourceless(
131: 				tfdiags.Error,
132: 				"Cannot apply non-applyable plan",
133: 				`The given plan is not applyable. If this seems like a bug in Terraform, then please report it!`,
134: 			))
135: 			return nil, nil, diags
136: 		}
137: 	}
138: 
139: 	// The caller must provide values for all of the "apply-time variables"
140: 	// mentioned in the plan, and for no others because the others come from
141: 	// the plan itself.
142: 	diags = diags.Append(checkApplyTimeVariables(plan.ApplyTimeVariables, opts.SetVariables, config))
143: 
144: 	if diags.HasErrors() {
145: 		// If the apply request is invalid in some way then we'll bail out
146: 		// here before we do any real work.
147: 		return nil, nil, diags
148: 	}
149: 
150: 	for _, rc := range plan.Changes.Resources {
151: 		// Import is a no-op change during an apply (all the real action happens during the plan) but we'd
152: 		// like to show some helpful output that mirrors the way we show other changes.
153: 		if rc.Importing != nil {
154: 			hookResourceID := HookResourceIdentity{
155: 				Addr:         rc.Addr,
156: 				ProviderAddr: rc.ProviderAddr.Provider,
157: 			}
158: 			for _, h := range c.hooks {
159: 				// In future, we may need to call PostApplyImport separately elsewhere in the apply
160: 				// operation. For now, though, we'll call Pre and Post hooks together.
161: 				h.PreApplyImport(hookResourceID, *rc.Importing)
162: 				h.PostApplyImport(hookResourceID, *rc.Importing)
163: 			}
164: 		}
165: 	}
166: 
167: 	graph, operation, moreDiags := c.applyGraph(plan, config, opts, true)
168: 	diags = diags.Append(moreDiags)
169: 	if moreDiags.HasErrors() {
170: 		return nil, nil, diags
171: 	}
172: 
173: 	moreDiags = checkExternalProviders(config, plan, nil, opts.ExternalProviders)
174: 	diags = diags.Append(moreDiags)
175: 	if moreDiags.HasErrors() {
176: 		return nil, nil, diags
177: 	}
178: 
179: 	schemas, schemaDiags := c.Schemas(config, plan.PriorState)
180: 	diags = diags.Append(schemaDiags)
181: 	if diags.HasErrors() {
182: 		return nil, nil, diags
183: 	}
184: 
185: 	changes, err := plan.Changes.Decode(schemas)
186: 	if err != nil {
187: 		diags = diags.Append(err)
188: 		return nil, nil, diags
189: 	}
190: 
191: 	workingState := plan.PriorState.DeepCopy()
192: 	walker, walkDiags := c.walk(graph, operation, &graphWalkOpts{
193: 		Config:                  config,
194: 		InputState:              workingState,
195: 		Changes:                 changes,
196: 		Overrides:               plan.Overrides,
197: 		ExternalProviderConfigs: opts.ExternalProviders,
198: 
199: 		DeferralAllowed: true,
200: 
201: 		// We need to propagate the check results from the plan phase,
202: 		// because that will tell us which checkable objects we're expecting
203: 		// to see updated results from during the apply step.
204: 		PlanTimeCheckResults: plan.Checks,
205: 
206: 		// We also want to propagate the timestamp from the plan file.
207: 		PlanTimeTimestamp: plan.Timestamp,
208: 
209: 		FunctionResults: lang.NewFunctionResultsTable(plan.FunctionResults),
210: 	})
211: 	diags = diags.Append(walker.NonFatalDiagnostics)
212: 	diags = diags.Append(walkDiags)
213: 
214: 	// After the walk is finished, we capture a simplified snapshot of the
215: 	// check result data as part of the new state.
216: 	walker.State.RecordCheckResults(walker.Checks)
217: 
218: 	newState := walker.State.Close()
219: 	if plan.UIMode == plans.DestroyMode && !diags.HasErrors() {
220: 		// NOTE: This is a vestigial violation of the rule that we mustn't
221: 		// use plan.UIMode to affect apply-time behavior.
222: 		// We ideally ought to just call newState.PruneResourceHusks
223: 		// unconditionally here, but we historically didn't and haven't yet
224: 		// verified that it'd be safe to do so.
225: 		newState.PruneResourceHusks()
226: 	}
227: 
228: 	if len(plan.TargetAddrs) > 0 {
229: 		diags = diags.Append(tfdiags.Sourceless(
230: 			tfdiags.Warning,
231: 			"Applied changes may be incomplete",
232: 			`The plan was created with the -target option in effect, so some changes requested in the configuration may have been ignored and the output values may not be fully updated. Run the following command to verify that no other changes are pending:
233:     terraform plan
234: 
235: Note that the -target option is not suitable for routine use, and is provided only for exceptional situations such as recovering from errors or mistakes, or when Terraform specifically suggests to use it as part of an error message.`,
236: 		))
237: 	}
238: 
239: 	// FIXME: we cannot check for an empty plan for refresh-only, because root
240: 	// outputs are always stored as changes. The final condition of the state
241: 	// also depends on some cleanup which happens during the apply walk. It
242: 	// would probably make more sense if applying a refresh-only plan were
243: 	// simply just returning the planned state and checks, but some extra
244: 	// cleanup is going to be needed to make the plan state match what apply
245: 	// would do. For now we can copy the checks over which were overwritten
246: 	// during the apply walk.
247: 	// Despite the intent of UIMode, it must still be used for apply-time
248: 	// differences in destroy plans too, so we can make use of that here as
249: 	// well.
250: 	if plan.UIMode == plans.RefreshOnlyMode {
251: 		newState.CheckResults = plan.Checks.DeepCopy()
252: 	}
253: 
254: 	// The caller also gets access to an expression evaluation scope in the
255: 	// root module, in case it needs to extract other information using
256: 	// expressions, like in "terraform console" or the test harness.
257: 	evalScope := evalScopeFromGraphWalk(walker, addrs.RootModuleInstance)
258: 
259: 	return newState, evalScope, diags
260: }
261: 
262: func checkApplyTimeVariables(needed collections.Set[string], gotValues InputValues, config *configs.Config) tfdiags.Diagnostics {
263: 	var diags tfdiags.Diagnostics
264: 	for name := range needed.All() {
265: 		if vv, exists := gotValues[name]; !exists || vv.Value == cty.NilVal || vv.Value.IsNull() {
266: 			// This error message assumes that the only possible reason for
267: 			// an apply-time variable is because the variable is ephemeral,
268: 			// which is true at the time of writing. This error message might
269: 			// need to be generalized if we introduce other reasons for
270: 			// apply-time variables in future.
271: 			diags = diags.Append(tfdiags.Sourceless(
272: 				tfdiags.Error,
273: 				"No value for required variable",
274: 				fmt.Sprintf("The ephemeral input variable %q was set during the plan phase, and so must also be set during the apply phase.", name),
275: 			))
276: 		}
277: 	}
278: 	for name := range gotValues {
279: 		if !needed.Has(name) {
280: 			// We'll treat this a little differently depending on whether
281: 			// the variable is declared as ephemeral or not.
282: 			if vc, ok := config.Module.Variables[name]; ok && vc.Ephemeral {
283: 				diags = diags.Append(tfdiags.Sourceless(
284: 					tfdiags.Error,
285: 					"No value for required variable",
286: 					fmt.Sprintf("The ephemeral input variable %q was not set during the plan phase, and so must remain unset during the apply phase.", name),
287: 				))
288: 			} else {
289: 				diags = diags.Append(tfdiags.Sourceless(
290: 					tfdiags.Error,
291: 					"Unexpected new value for variable",
292: 					fmt.Sprintf("Input variable %q is non-ephemeral, so its value was fixed during planning and cannot be reset during apply.", name),
293: 				))
294: 			}
295: 		}
296: 	}
297: 	return diags
298: }
299: 
300: func (c *Context) applyGraph(plan *plans.Plan, config *configs.Config, opts *ApplyOpts, validate bool) (*Graph, walkOperation, tfdiags.Diagnostics) {
301: 	var diags tfdiags.Diagnostics
302: 
303: 	if opts == nil {
304: 		opts = new(ApplyOpts)
305: 	}
306: 
307: 	variables := InputValues{}
308: 	for name, dyVal := range plan.VariableValues {
309: 		val, err := dyVal.Decode(cty.DynamicPseudoType)
310: 		if err != nil {
311: 			diags = diags.Append(tfdiags.Sourceless(
312: 				tfdiags.Error,
313: 				"Invalid variable value in plan",
314: 				fmt.Sprintf("Invalid value for variable %q recorded in plan file: %s.", name, err),
315: 			))
316: 			continue
317: 		}
318: 		if pvm, ok := plan.VariableMarks[name]; ok {
319: 			val = val.MarkWithPaths(pvm)
320: 		}
321: 
322: 		variables[name] = &InputValue{
323: 			Value:      val,
324: 			SourceType: ValueFromPlan,
325: 		}
326: 	}
327: 	// Apply-time variables need to be merged in too.
328: 	// FIXME: We should check that all of these match declared variables and
329: 	// that all of them are declared as ephemeral, because all non-ephemeral
330: 	// variables are supposed to come exclusively from plan.VariableValues.
331: 	for n, vv := range opts.SetVariables {
332: 		variables[n] = vv
333: 	}
334: 	if diags.HasErrors() {
335: 		return nil, walkApply, diags
336: 	}
337: 
338: 	// The plan.VariableValues field only records variables that were actually
339: 	// set by the caller in the PlanOpts, so we may need to provide
340: 	// placeholders for any other variables that the user didn't set, in
341: 	// which case Terraform will once again use the default value from the
342: 	// configuration when we visit these variables during the graph walk.
343: 	for name := range config.Module.Variables {
344: 		if _, ok := variables[name]; ok {
345: 			continue
346: 		}
347: 		variables[name] = &InputValue{
348: 			Value:      cty.NilVal,
349: 			SourceType: ValueFromPlan,
350: 		}
351: 	}
352: 
353: 	operation := walkApply
354: 	if plan.UIMode == plans.DestroyMode {
355: 		// FIXME: Due to differences in how objects must be handled in the
356: 		// graph and evaluated during a complete destroy, we must continue to
357: 		// use plans.DestroyMode to switch on this behavior. If all objects
358: 		// which require special destroy handling can be tracked in the plan,
359: 		// then this switch will no longer be needed and we can remove the
360: 		// walkDestroy operation mode.
361: 		// TODO: Audit that and remove walkDestroy as an operation mode.
362: 		operation = walkDestroy
363: 	}
364: 
365: 	graph, moreDiags := (&ApplyGraphBuilder{
366: 		Config:                    config,
367: 		Changes:                   plan.Changes,
368: 		DeferredChanges:           plan.DeferredResources,
369: 		State:                     plan.PriorState,
370: 		RootVariableValues:        variables,
371: 		ExternalProviderConfigs:   opts.ExternalProviders,
372: 		Plugins:                   c.plugins,
373: 		Targets:                   plan.TargetAddrs,
374: 		ActionTargets:             plan.ActionTargetAddrs,
375: 		ForceReplace:              plan.ForceReplaceAddrs,
376: 		Operation:                 operation,
377: 		ExternalReferences:        plan.ExternalReferences,
378: 		Overrides:                 plan.Overrides,
379: 		SkipGraphValidation:       c.graphOpts.SkipGraphValidation,
380: 		AllowRootEphemeralOutputs: opts.AllowRootEphemeralOutputs,
381: 	}).Build(addrs.RootModuleInstance)
382: 	diags = diags.Append(moreDiags)
383: 	if moreDiags.HasErrors() {
384: 		return nil, walkApply, diags
385: 	}
386: 
387: 	return graph, operation, diags
388: }
389: 
390: // ApplyGraphForUI is a last vestage of graphs in the public interface of
391: // Context (as opposed to graphs as an implementation detail) intended only for
392: // use by the "terraform graph" command when asked to render an apply-time
393: // graph.
394: //
395: // The result of this is intended only for rendering ot the user as a dot
396: // graph, and so may change in future in order to make the result more useful
397: // in that context, even if drifts away from the physical graph that Terraform
398: // Core currently uses as an implementation detail of applying.
399: func (c *Context) ApplyGraphForUI(plan *plans.Plan, config *configs.Config) (*Graph, tfdiags.Diagnostics) {
400: 	// For now though, this really is just the internal graph, confusing
401: 	// implementation details and all.
402: 
403: 	var diags tfdiags.Diagnostics
404: 
405: 	graph, _, moreDiags := c.applyGraph(plan, config, nil, false)
406: 	diags = diags.Append(moreDiags)
407: 	return graph, diags
408: }

{"content":"1: // Copyright (c) HashiCorp, Inc.
2: // SPDX-License-Identifier: BUSL-1.1
3: 
4: package terraform
5: 
6: import (
7: 	"fmt"
8: 	"log"
9: 	"sort"
10: 
11: 	"github.com/hashicorp/hcl/v2"
12: 
13: 	"github.com/hashicorp/terraform/internal/addrs"
14: 	"github.com/hashicorp/terraform/internal/configs/configschema"
15: 	"github.com/hashicorp/terraform/internal/dag"
16: 	"github.com/hashicorp/terraform/internal/lang/langrefs"
17: )
18: 
19: // GraphNodeReferenceable must be implemented by any node that represents
20: // a Terraform thing that can be referenced (resource, module, etc.).
21: //
22: // Even if the thing has no name, this should return an empty list. By
23: // implementing this and returning a non-nil result, you say that this CAN
24: // be referenced and other methods of referencing may still be possible (such
25: // as by path!)
26: type GraphNodeReferenceable interface {
27: 	GraphNodeModulePath
28: 
29: 	// ReferenceableAddrs returns a list of addresses through which this can be
30: 	// referenced.
31: 	ReferenceableAddrs() []addrs.Referenceable
32: }
33: 
34: // GraphNodeReferencer must be implemented by nodes that reference other
35: // Terraform items and therefore depend on them.
36: type GraphNodeReferencer interface {
37: 	GraphNodeModulePath
38: 
39: 	// References returns a list of references made by this node, which
40: 	// include both a referenced address and source location information for
41: 	// the reference.
42: 	References() []*addrs.Reference
43: }
44: 
45: // GraphNodeReferencer must be implemented by nodes that import resources.
46: type GraphNodeImportReferencer interface {
47: 	GraphNodeReferencer
48: 
49: 	// ImportReferences returns a list of references made by this node's
50: 	// associated import block.
51: 	ImportReferences() []*addrs.Reference
52: }
53: 
54: type GraphNodeAttachDependencies interface {
55: 	AttachDependencies([]addrs.ConfigResource)
56: }
57: 
58: // graphNodeDependsOn is implemented by resources that need to expose any
59: // references set via DependsOn in their configuration.
60: type graphNodeDependsOn interface {
61: 	GraphNodeReferencer
62: 	DependsOn() []*addrs.Reference
63: }
64: 
65: // graphNodeAttachDataResourceDependsOn records all resources that are transitively
66: // referenced through depends_on in the configuration. This is used by data
67: // resources to determine if they can be read during the plan, or if they need
68: // to be further delayed until apply.
69: // We can only use an addrs.ConfigResource address here, because modules are
70: // not yet expended in the graph. While this will cause some extra data
71: // resources to show in the plan when their depends_on references may be in
72: // unrelated module instances, the fact that it only happens when there are any
73: // resource updates pending means we can still avoid the problem of the
74: // "perpetual diff"
75: type graphNodeAttachDataResourceDependsOn interface {
76: 	GraphNodeConfigResource
77: 	graphNodeDependsOn
78: 
79: 	// AttachDataResourceDependsOn stores the discovered dependencies in the
80: 	// resource node for evaluation later.
81: 	AttachDataResourceDependsOn(deps []addrs.ConfigResource)
82: }
83: 
84: // GraphNodeReferenceOutside is an interface that can optionally be implemented.
85: // A node that implements it can specify that its own referenceable addresses
86: // and/or the addresses it references are in a different module than the
87: // node itself.
88: //
89: // Any referenceable addresses returned by ReferenceableAddrs are interpreted
90: // relative to the returned selfPath.
91: //
92: // Any references returned by References are interpreted relative to the
93: // returned referencePath.
94: //
95: // It is valid but not required for either of these paths to match what is
96: // returned by method Path, though if both match the main Path then there
97: // is no reason to implement this method.
98: //
99: // The primary use-case for this is the nodes representing module input
100: // variables, since their expressions are resolved in terms of their calling
101: // module, but they are still referenced from their own module.
102: type GraphNodeReferenceOutside interface {
103: 	// ReferenceOutside returns a path in which any references from this node
104: 	// are resolved.
105: 	ReferenceOutside() (selfPath, referencePath addrs.Module)
106: }
107: 
108: // ReferenceTransformer is a GraphTransformer that connects all the
109: // nodes that reference each other in order to form the proper ordering.
110: type ReferenceTransformer struct{}
111: 
112: func (t *ReferenceTransformer) Transform(g *Graph) error {
113: 	// Build a reference map so we can efficiently look up the references
114: 	vs := g.Vertices()
115: 	m := NewReferenceMap(vs)
116: 
117: 	// Find the things that reference things and connect them
118: 	for _, v := range vs {
119: 		if _, ok := v.(GraphNodeDestroyer); ok {
120: 			// destroy nodes references are not connected, since they can only
121: 			// use their own state.
122: 			continue
123: 		}
124: 
125: 		parents := m.References(v)
126: 		parentsDbg := make([]string, len(parents))
127: 		for i, v := range parents {
128: 			parentsDbg[i] = dag.VertexName(v)
129: 		}
130: 		log.Printf(
131: 			"[DEBUG] ReferenceTransformer: %q references: %v",
132: 			dag.VertexName(v), parentsDbg)
133: 
134: 		for _, parent := range parents {
135: 			// A destroy plan relies solely on the state, so we only need to
136: 			// ensure that temporary values are connected to get the evaluation
137: 			// order correct. Any references to destroy nodes will cause
138: 			// cycles, because they are connected in reverse order.
139: 			if _, ok := parent.(GraphNodeDestroyer); ok {
140: 				continue
141: 			}
142: 
143: 			if !graphNodesAreResourceInstancesInDifferentInstancesOfSameModule(v, parent) {
144: 				g.Connect(dag.BasicEdge(v, parent))
145: 			} else {
146: 				log.Printf("[TRACE] ReferenceTransformer: skipping %s => %s inter-module-instance dependency", dag.VertexName(v), dag.VertexName(parent))
147: 			}
148: 		}
149: 
150: 		if len(parents) > 0 {
151: 			continue
152: 		}
153: 	}
154: 
155: 	return nil
156: }
157: 
158: type depMap map[string]addrs.ConfigResource
159: 
160: // add stores the vertex if it represents a resource in the
161: // graph.
162: func (m depMap) add(v dag.Vertex) {
163: 	// we're only concerned with resources which may have changes that
164: 	// need to be applied.
165: 	switch v := v.(type) {
166: 	case GraphNodeResourceInstance:
167: 		instAddr := v.ResourceInstanceAddr()
168: 		addr := instAddr.ContainingResource().Config()
169: 		m[addr.String()] = addr
170: 	case GraphNodeConfigResource:
171: 		addr := v.ResourceAddr()
172: 		m[addr.String()] = addr
173: 	}
174: }
175: 
176: // attachDataResourceDependsOnTransformer records all resources transitively
177: // referenced through a configuration depends_on.
178: type attachDataResourceDependsOnTransformer struct {
179: }
180: 
181: func (t attachDataResourceDependsOnTransformer) Transform(g *Graph) error {
182: 	// First we need to make a map of referenceable addresses to their vertices.
183: 	// This is very similar to what's done in ReferenceTransformer, but we keep
184: 	// implementation separate as they may need to change independently.
185: 	vertices := g.Vertices()
186: 	refMap := NewReferenceMap(vertices)
187: 
188: 	for _, v := range vertices {
189: 		depender, ok := v.(graphNodeAttachDataResourceDependsOn)
190: 		if !ok {
191: 			continue
192: 		}
193: 
194: 		// Only data need to attach depends_on, so they can determine if they
195: 		// are eligible to be read during plan.
196: 		if depender.ResourceAddr().Resource.Mode != addrs.DataResourceMode {
197: 			continue
198: 		}
199: 
200: 		// depMap will only add resource references then dedupe
201: 		deps := make(depMap)
202: 		dependsOnDeps := refMap.dependsOn(g, depender)
203: 		for _, dep := range dependsOnDeps {
204: 			// any the dependency
205: 			deps.add(dep)
206: 		}
207: 
208: 		res := make([]addrs.ConfigResource, 0, len(deps))
209: 		for _, d := range deps {
210: 			res = append(res, d)
211: 		}
212: 
213: 		log.Printf("[TRACE] attachDataDependenciesTransformer: %s depends on %s", depender.ResourceAddr(), res)
214: 		depender.AttachDataResourceDependsOn(res)
215: 	}
216: 
217: 	return nil
218: }
219: 
220: // AttachDependenciesTransformer records all resource dependencies for each
221: // instance, and attaches the addresses to the node itself. Managed resource
222: // will record these in the state for proper ordering of destroy operations.
223: type AttachDependenciesTransformer struct {
224: }
225: 
226: func (t AttachDependenciesTransformer) Transform(g *Graph) error {
227: 	for _, v := range g.Vertices() {
228: 		attacher, ok := v.(GraphNodeAttachDependencies)
229: 		if !ok {
230: 			continue
231: 		}
232: 
233: 		// We'll check if the node is a config resource already, in which case
234: 		// we want to make sure it is not referencing itself.
235: 
236: 		// matchesSelf is a function that returns true if the given address
237: 		// matches the address of the node itself.
238: 		matchesSelf := func(addrs.ConfigResource) bool {
239: 			// Default case is to always return false.
240: 			return false
241: 		}
242: 
243: 		self, ok := v.(GraphNodeConfigResource)
244: 		if ok {
245: 			matchesSelf = func(addr addrs.ConfigResource) bool {
246: 				// If we know the node is a config resource, we can compare
247: 				// the addresses directly.
248: 				return addr.Equal(self.ResourceAddr())
249: 			}
250: 		}
251: 
252: 		// dedupe addrs when there's multiple instances involved, or
253: 		// multiple paths in the un-reduced graph
254: 		depMap := map[string]addrs.ConfigResource{}
255: 
256: 		// since we need to type-switch over the nodes anyway, we're going to
257: 		// insert the address directly into depMap and forget about the returned
258: 		// set.
259: 		for _, d := range g.Ancestors(v) {
260: 			var addr addrs.ConfigResource
261: 
262: 			switch d := d.(type) {
263: 			case GraphNodeResourceInstance:
264: 				instAddr := d.ResourceInstanceAddr()
265: 				addr = instAddr.ContainingResource().Config()
266: 			case GraphNodeConfigResource:
267: 				addr = d.ResourceAddr()
268: 			default:
269: 				continue
270: 			}
271: 
272: 			if matchesSelf(addr) {
273: 				continue
274: 			}
275: 
276: 			depMap[addr.String()] = addr
277: 		}
278: 
279: 		deps := make([]addrs.ConfigResource, 0, len(depMap))
280: 		for _, d := range depMap {
281: 			deps = append(deps, d)
282: 		}
283: 		sort.Slice(deps, func(i, j int) bool {
284: 			return deps[i].String() < deps[j].String()
285: 		})
286: 
287: 		log.Printf("[TRACE] AttachDependenciesTransformer: %s depends on %s", dag.VertexName(v), deps)
288: 		attacher.AttachDependencies(deps)
289: 	}
290: 
291: 	return nil
292: }
293: 
294: func isDependableResource(v dag.Vertex) bool {
295: 	switch v.(type) {
296: 	case GraphNodeResourceInstance:
297: 		return true
298: 	case GraphNodeConfigResource:
299: 		return true
300: 	}
301: 	return false
302: }
303: 
304: // ReferenceMap is a structure that can be used to efficiently check
305: // for references on a graph, mapping internal reference keys (as produced by
306: // the mapKey method) to one or more vertices that are identified by each key.
307: type ReferenceMap map[string][]dag.Vertex
308: 
309: // References returns the set of vertices that the given vertex refers to,
310: // and any referenced addresses that do not have corresponding vertices.
311: func (m ReferenceMap) References(v dag.Vertex) []dag.Vertex {
312: 	var matches []dag.Vertex
313: 	var referenceKeys []string
314: 
315: 	if rn, ok := v.(GraphNodeReferencer); ok {
316: 		for _, ref := range rn.References() {
317: 			referenceKeys = append(referenceKeys, m.referenceMapKey(vertexReferencePath(v), ref.Subject))
318: 
319: 		}
320: 	}
321: 
322: 	if rn, ok := v.(GraphNodeImportReferencer); ok {
323: 		for _, ref := range rn.ImportReferences() {
324: 			// import block references are always in the root module scope
325: 			referenceKeys = append(referenceKeys, m.referenceMapKey(addrs.RootModule, ref.Subject))
326: 		}
327: 	}
328: 
329: 	for _, key := range referenceKeys {
330: 		vertices := m[key]
331: 		for _, rv := range vertices {
332: 			// don't include self-references
333: 			if rv == v {
334: 				continue
335: 			}
336: 			matches = append(matches, rv)
337: 		}
338: 	}
339: 	return matches
340: }
341: 
342: // dependsOn returns the set of vertices that the given vertex refers to from
343: // the configured depends_on. This is only used to calculate depends_on for
344: // data sources. No other resource type changes it's behavior based on how
345: // dependencies are declared, hence everything else is resolved via the normal
346: // reference mechanism.
347: func (m ReferenceMap) dependsOn(g *Graph, depender graphNodeDependsOn) []dag.Vertex {
348: 	res := make(dag.Set)
349: 
350: 	refs := depender.DependsOn()
351: 
352: 	// get any implied dependencies for data sources
353: 	refs = append(refs, m.dataDependsOn(depender)...)
354: 
355: 	for _, ref := range refs {
356: 		subject := ref.Subject
357: 
358: 		key := m.referenceMapKey(vertexReferencePath(depender), subject)
359: 		vertices, ok := m[key]
360: 		if !ok {
361: 			// the ReferenceMap generates all possible keys, so any warning
362: 			// here is probably not useful for this implementation.
363: 			continue
364: 		}
365: 		for _, rv := range vertices {
366: 			// don't include self-references
367: 			if rv == depender {
368: 				continue
369: 			}
370: 			res.Add(rv)
371: 
372: 			// Check any ancestors for transitive dependencies when we're not
373: 			// pointed directly at a resource. We can't be much more precise
374: 			// here, since in order to maintain our guarantee that data sources
375: 			// will wait for explicit dependencies, if those dependencies happen
376: 			// to be a module, output, or variable, we have to find some
377: 			// upstream managed resource in order to check for a planned change.
378: 			// We need to descend through all ancestors here, because data
379: 			// sources aren't just tracking this for graph edges, but rather
380: 			// they need to look for changes during the plan.
381: 			if _, ok := rv.(GraphNodeConfigResource); !ok {
382: 				for _, v := range g.Ancestors(rv) {
383: 					if isDependableResource(v) {
384: 						res.Add(v)
385: 					}
386: 				}
387: 			}
388: 		}
389: 	}
390: 
391: 	parentDeps := m.parentModuleDependsOn(g, depender)
392: 	// dag.Set doesn't have an insert/union method, but they are simple maps
393: 	for k, v := range parentDeps {
394: 		res[k] = v
395: 	}
396: 
397: 	// Now we need to convert the set back to our slice type, because Set.List()
398: 	// returns []any.
399: 	vertices := make([]dag.Vertex, 0, res.Len())
400: 	for _, v := range res {
401: 		vertices = append(vertices, v)
402: 	}
403: 	return vertices
404: }
405: 
406: // Return extra depends_on references if this is a data source.
407: // For data sources we implicitly treat references to managed resources as
408: // depends_on entries. If a data source references a managed resource, even if
409: // that reference is resolvable, it stands to reason that the user intends for
410: // the data source to require that resource in some way.
411: func (m ReferenceMap) dataDependsOn(depender graphNodeDependsOn) []*addrs.Reference {
412: 	var refs []*addrs.Reference
413: 	if n, ok := depender.(GraphNodeConfigResource); ok &&
414: 		n.ResourceAddr().Resource.Mode == addrs.DataResourceMode {
415: 		for _, r := range depender.References() {
416: 
417: 			var resAddr addrs.Resource
418: 			switch s := r.Subject.(type) {
419: 			case addrs.Resource:
420: 				resAddr = s
421: 			case addrs.ResourceInstance:
422: 				resAddr = s.Resource
423: 				r.Subject = resAddr
424: 			}
425: 
426: 			if resAddr.Mode != addrs.ManagedResourceMode {
427: 				// We only want to wait on directly referenced managed resources.
428: 				// Data sources have no external side effects, so normal
429: 				// references to them in the config will suffice for proper
430: 				// ordering.
431: 				continue
432: 			}
433: 
434: 			refs = append(refs, r)
435: 		}
436: 	}
437: 	return refs
438: }
439: 
440: // parentModuleDependsOn returns the set of vertices that a data sources parent
441: // module references through the module call's depends_on.
442: func (m ReferenceMap) parentModuleDependsOn(g *Graph, depender graphNodeDependsOn) dag.Set {
443: 	res := make(dag.Set)
444: 
445: 	// Look for containing modules with DependsOn.
446: 	// This should be connected directly to the module node, so we only need to
447: 	// look one step away.
448: 	for _, v := range g.DownEdges(depender) {
449: 		// we're only concerned with module expansion nodes here.
450: 		mod, ok := v.(*nodeExpandModule)
451: 		if !ok {
452: 			continue
453: 		}
454: 
455: 		deps := m.dependsOn(g, mod)
456: 		for _, dep := range deps {
457: 			if isDependableResource(dep) {
458: 				res.Add(dep)
459: 			}
460: 		}
461: 
462: 		// We need to descend through all ancestors here, because data sources
463: 		// aren't just tracking this for graph edges, but rather they need to
464: 		// look for changes during the plan.
465: 		for _, v := range g.Ancestors(deps...) {
466: 			if isDependableResource(v) {
467: 				res.Add(v)
468: 			}
469: 		}
470: 	}
471: 
472: 	return res
473: }
474: 
475: func (m *ReferenceMap) mapKey(path addrs.Module, addr addrs.Referenceable) string {
476: 	return fmt.Sprintf("%s|%s", path.String(), addr.String())
477: }
478: 
479: // vertexReferenceablePath returns the path in which the given vertex can be
480: // referenced. This is the path that its results from ReferenceableAddrs
481: // are considered to be relative to.
482: //
483: // Only GraphNodeModulePath implementations can be referenced, so this method will
484: // panic if the given vertex does not implement that interface.
485: func vertexReferenceablePath(v dag.Vertex) addrs.Module {
486: 	sp, ok := v.(GraphNodeModulePath)
487: 	if !ok {
488: 		// Only nodes with paths can participate in a reference map.
489: 		panic(fmt.Errorf("vertexMapKey on vertex type %T which doesn't implement GraphNodeModulePath", sp))
490: 	}
491: 
492: 	if outside, ok := v.(GraphNodeReferenceOutside); ok {
493: 		// Vertex is referenced from a different module than where it was
494: 		// declared.
495: 		path, _ := outside.ReferenceOutside()
496: 		return path
497: 	}
498: 
499: 	// Vertex is referenced from the same module as where it was declared.
500: 	return sp.ModulePath()
501: }
502: 
503: // vertexReferencePath returns the path in which references _from_ the given
504: // vertex must be interpreted.
505: //
506: // Only GraphNodeModulePath implementations can have references, so this method
507: // will panic if the given vertex does not implement that interface.
508: func vertexReferencePath(v dag.Vertex) addrs.Module {
509: 	sp, ok := v.(GraphNodeModulePath)
510: 	if !ok {
511: 		// Only nodes with paths can participate in a reference map.
512: 		panic(fmt.Errorf("vertexReferencePath on vertex type %T which doesn't implement GraphNodeModulePath", v))
513: 	}
514: 
515: 	if outside, ok := v.(GraphNodeReferenceOutside); ok {
516: 		// Vertex makes references to objects in a different module than where
517: 		// it was declared.
518: 		_, path := outside.ReferenceOutside()
519: 		return path
520: 	}
521: 
522: 	// Vertex makes references to objects in the same module as where it
523: 	// was declared.
524: 	return sp.ModulePath()
525: }
526: 
527: // referenceMapKey produces keys for the "edges" map. "referrer" is the vertex
528: // that the reference is from, and "addr" is the address of the object being
529: // referenced.
530: //
531: // The result is an opaque string that includes both the address of the given
532: // object and the address of the module instance that object belongs to.
533: //
534: // Only GraphNodeModulePath implementations can be referrers, so this method will
535: // panic if the given vertex does not implement that interface.
536: func (m ReferenceMap) referenceMapKey(path addrs.Module, addr addrs.Referenceable) string {
537: 	key := m.mapKey(path, addr)
538: 	if _, exists := m[key]; !exists {
539: 		// If what we were looking for was a ResourceInstance then we
540: 		// might be in a resource-oriented graph rather than an
541: 		// instance-oriented graph, and so we'll see if we have the
542: 		// resource itself instead.
543: 
544: 		if ri, ok := addr.(addrs.ResourceInstance); ok {
545: 			return m.mapKey(path, ri.ContainingResource())
546: 		}
547: 
548: 		if rip, ok := addr.(addrs.ResourceInstancePhase); ok {
549: 			return m.mapKey(path, rip.ContainingResource())
550: 		}
551: 
552: 		if mcio, ok := addr.(addrs.ModuleCallInstanceOutput); ok {
553: 
554: 			// A module call instance output is a reference to an output of a
555: 			// specific module call. If we can't find that, we'll look first
556: 			// for the general non-instanced output.
557: 
558: 			key = m.mapKey(path, mcio.ModuleCallOutput())
559: 			if _, exists := m[key]; exists {
560: 				// We found it, so we can just use that.
561: 				return key
562: 			}
563: 
564: 			// Otherwise we'll look just for the instanced module call itself.
565: 
566: 			key = m.mapKey(path, mcio.Call)
567: 			if _, exists := m[key]; exists {
568: 				// We found it, so we can just use that.
569: 				return key
570: 			}
571: 
572: 			// If we still can't find it, then we'll look for the non-instanced
573: 			// module call. This is the same as we'd do if the original call had
574: 			// just been for a ModuleCallInstance, so we'll let that fall
575: 			// through.
576: 
577: 			addr = mcio.Call
578: 
579: 		}
580: 
581: 		if mci, ok := addr.(addrs.ModuleCallInstance); ok {
582: 			return m.mapKey(path, mci.Call)
583: 		}
584: 
585: 		if ai, ok := addr.(addrs.ActionInstance); ok {
586: 			return m.mapKey(path, ai.ContainingAction())
587: 		}
588: 
589: 		// If nothing matched, then we'll just return the original key
590: 		// unchanged.
591: 	}
592: 	return key
593: }
594: 
595: // NewReferenceMap is used to create a new reference map for the
596: // given set of vertices.
597: func NewReferenceMap(vs []dag.Vertex) ReferenceMap {
598: 	// Build the lookup table
599: 	m := make(ReferenceMap)
600: 	for _, v := range vs {
601: 		// We're only looking for referenceable nodes
602: 		rn, ok := v.(GraphNodeReferenceable)
603: 		if !ok {
604: 			continue
605: 		}
606: 
607: 		path := vertexReferenceablePath(v)
608: 
609: 		// Go through and cache them
610: 		for _, addr := range rn.ReferenceableAddrs() {
611: 			key := m.mapKey(path, addr)
612: 			m[key] = append(m[key], v)
613: 		}
614: 	}
615: 
616: 	return m
617: }
618: 
619: // ReferencesFromConfig returns the references that a configuration has
620: // based on the interpolated variables in a configuration.
621: func ReferencesFromConfig(body hcl.Body, schema *configschema.Block) []*addrs.Reference {
622: 	if body == nil {
623: 		return nil
624: 	}
625: 	refs, _ := langrefs.ReferencesInBlock(addrs.ParseRef, body, schema)
626: 	return refs
627: }

31: 
32: type hookFunc func(func(Hook) (HookAction, error)) error
33: 
34: // EvalContext is the interface that is given to eval nodes to execute.
35: type EvalContext interface {
36: 	// Stopped returns a context that is canceled when evaluation is stopped via
37: 	// Terraform.Context.Stop()
38: 	StopCtx() context.Context
39: 
40: 	// Path is the current module path.
41: 	Path() addrs.ModuleInstance
42: 
43: 	// Hook is used to call hook methods. The callback is called for each
44: 	// hook and should return the hook action to take and the error.
45: 	Hook(func(Hook) (HookAction, error)) error
46: 
47: 	// Input is the UIInput object for interacting with the UI.
48: 	Input() UIInput
49: 
50: 	// InitProvider initializes the provider with the given address, and returns
51: 	// the implementation of the resource provider or an error.
52: 	//
53: 	// It is an error to initialize the same provider more than once. This
54: 	// method will panic if the module instance address of the given provider
55: 	// configuration does not match the Path() of the EvalContext.
56: 	InitProvider(addr addrs.AbsProviderConfig, configs *configs.Provider) (providers.Interface, error)
57: 
58: 	// Provider gets the provider instance with the given address (already
59: 	// initialized) or returns nil if the provider isn't initialized.
60: 	//
61: 	// This method expects an _absolute_ provider configuration address, since
62: 	// resources in one module are able to use providers from other modules.
63: 	// InitProvider must've been called on the EvalContext of the module
64: 	// that owns the given provider before calling this method.
65: 	Provider(addrs.AbsProviderConfig) providers.Interface
66: 
67: 	// ProviderSchema retrieves the schema for a particular provider, which
68: 	// must have already been initialized with InitProvider.
69: 	//
70: 	// This method expects an _absolute_ provider configuration address, since
71: 	// resources in one module are able to use providers from other modules.
72: 	ProviderSchema(addrs.AbsProviderConfig) (providers.ProviderSchema, error)
73: 
74: 	// CloseProvider closes provider connections that aren't needed anymore.
75: 	//
76: 	// This method will panic if the module instance address of the given
77: 	// provider configuration does not match the Path() of the EvalContext.
78: 	CloseProvider(addrs.AbsProviderConfig) error
79: 
80: 	// ConfigureProvider configures the provider with the given
81: 	// configuration. This is a separate context call because this call

16: // State is the top-level type of a Terraform state.
17: //
18: // A state should be mutated only via its accessor methods, to ensure that
19: // invariants are preserved.
20: //
21: // Access to State and the nested values within it is not concurrency-safe,
22: // so when accessing a State object concurrently it is the caller's
23: // responsibility to ensure that only one write is in progress at a time
24: // and that reads only occur when no write is in progress. The most common
25: // way to achieve this is to wrap the State in a SyncState and use the
26: // higher-level atomic operations supported by that type.
27: type State struct {
28: 	// Modules contains the state for each module. The keys in this map are
29: 	// an implementation detail and must not be used by outside callers.
30: 	Modules map[string]*Module
31: 
32: 	// OutputValues contains the state for each output value defined in the
33: 	// root module.
34: 	//
35: 	// Output values in other modules don't persist anywhere between runs,
36: 	// so Terraform Core tracks those only internally and does not expose
37: 	// them in any artifacts that survive between runs.
38: 	RootOutputValues map[string]*OutputValue
39: 
40: 	// CheckResults contains a snapshot of the statuses of checks at the
41: 	// end of the most recent update to the state. Callers might compare
42: 	// checks between runs to see if e.g. a previously-failing check has
43: 	// been fixed since the last run, or similar.
44: 	//
45: 	// CheckResults can be nil to indicate that there are no check results
46: 	// from the previous run at all, which is subtly different than the
47: 	// previous run having affirmatively recorded that there are no checks
48: 	// to run. For example, if this object was created from a state snapshot
49: 	// created by a version of Terraform that didn't yet support checks
50: 	// then this field will be nil.
51: 	CheckResults *CheckResults
52: }
53: 
54: // NewState constructs a minimal empty state, containing an empty root module.
55: func NewState() *State {
56: 	modules := map[string]*Module{}
57: 	modules[addrs.RootModuleInstance.String()] = NewModule(addrs.RootModuleInstance)
58: 	return &State{
59: 		Modules:          modules,
60: 		RootOutputValues: make(map[string]*OutputValue),
61: 	}
62: }
63: 
64: // BuildState is a helper -- primarily intended for tests -- to build a state
65: // using imperative code against the StateSync type while still acting as
66: // an expression of type *State to assign into a containing struct.
67: func BuildState(cb func(*SyncState)) *State {
68: 	s := NewState()
69: 	cb(s.SyncWrapper())
70: 	return s
71: }
72: 
73: // Empty returns true if there are no resources or populated output values
74: // in the receiver. In other words, if this state could be safely replaced
75: // with the return value of NewState and be functionally equivalent.
76: func (s *State) Empty() bool {
77: 	if s == nil {
78: 		return true
79: 	}
80: 	if len(s.RootOutputValues) != 0 {
81: 		return false
82: 	}
83: 	for _, ms := range s.Modules {

86: 		}
87: 	}
88: 	return true
89: }
